title: Advanced Excel Toolkit
subhead: The Excel skills that separate accountants who survive month-end from accountants who drown in it — data cleaning, lookups, conditional summing, PivotTables, and Power Query.
hook: "What's the first thing you do with a 5,000-row payroll export?" The wrong answer is "start vouching." The right answer is "clean it." Raw exports from payroll, bank feeds, and offshore timesheet apps are always messy, and cleaning is an accounting skill, not an IT one.

chunks.title: Timed speed drill — 10 journal entries, 60 seconds each
chunks.intro: Full mixed recall under pressure. 60 seconds per entry. Recall fast — your brain needs to retrieve journal entries without notes.

questions.q: Record a $7,200 offshore drafting invoice (no cash yet).
options: Dr 5100 COGS — Subcontractor Drafting / Cr 2000 Accounts Payable
questions.explanation: Expense + liability: Dr COGS $7,200 / Cr AP $7,200. Cash untouched until payment.

questions.q: Record a $36,000 annual software license payment (capitalize).
options: Dr 1400 Prepaid Software / Cr 1000 Operating Checking
questions.explanation: Capitalize as prepaid: Dr 1400 $36,000 / Cr 1000 $36,000. Expense it monthly later via amortization.

questions.q: Post January depreciation for 5 workstations @ $200/month each (total $1,000).
options: Dr 1590 Accumulated Depreciation / Cr 6700 Depreciation Expense
Dr 6700 Depreciation Expense / Cr 1590 Accumulated Depreciation
questions.explanation: Dr 6700 Depreciation Expense $1,000 / Cr 1590 Accumulated Depreciation $1,000.

options: Dr 6800 Interest Expense $2,708 / Cr 2300 Accrued Interest $2,708
questions.explanation: $500,000 × 6.5% ÷ 12 = $2,708.33. Dr Interest Expense / Cr Accrued Interest.

questions.q: Record the month-end amortization of Prepaid Software for one month ($3,000).
options: Dr 6500 Software & Subscriptions / Cr 1400 Prepaid Software
questions.explanation: Dr 6500 Software Expense $3,000 / Cr 1400 Prepaid Software $3,000 — move one month's benefit off the asset onto the P&L.

questions.q: Correct a duplicate $9,503.60 customer deposit (entered twice).
questions.explanation: Reverse the duplicate: Dr AR $9,503.60 / Cr Cash $9,503.60. Revenue was never overstated — only cash/AR were.

questions.q: Record a $480,000 AR invoice issued to Atlantic Fiber for an HLD milestone.
options: Dr 1100 AR $480,000 / Cr 4000 Engineering Design Revenue $480,000
questions.explanation: Dr AR / Cr Revenue — performance obligation satisfied at HLD milestone. No cash yet.

questions.q: Record Atlantic Fiber's wire payment of $479,975 (after $25 bank fee).
questions.explanation: Net cash $479,975 debited, $25 fee to expense, full $480,000 AR credited and cleared.

questions.q: Write off TeleCom Builders' $112,700 AR (against existing allowance).
options: Dr 6720 Bad Debt Expense / Cr 1100 AR
questions.explanation: Dr Allowance / Cr AR. Expense was already booked when the allowance was established — not touched at write-off.

questions.q: Establish the bad debt allowance for 1.5% of $997,700 AR.
options: Dr 6720 Bad Debt Expense $14,965.50 / Cr 1200 Allowance $14,965.50
questions.explanation: 1.5% × $997,700 = $14,965.50. Dr Bad Debt Expense / Cr Allowance for Doubtful Accounts.

chunks.title: Excel for Accountants — The 3 Functions That Do 80% of the Work
chunks.intro: If you can only learn three Excel functions before your first accounting job, learn these: VLOOKUP, SUMIFS, and PivotTables.
chunks.transcript:
0:00 — If you can only learn three Excel functions before your first accounting job, learn these: VLOOKUP, SUMIFS, and PivotTables. Everything else is optional. These three handle 80% of the real work you will actually do.

0:30 — VLOOKUP — or its modern replacement XLOOKUP — is how you match data from two tables. The most common accounting use: a 3,000-row expense report and a separate table of GL account numbers. VLOOKUP pulls the GL number into the right row automatically. Without it, you do that manually for 3,000 rows.

1:30 — SUMIFS adds up numbers that meet multiple conditions at once. "Give me total COGS for Tennessee Corridor, in January, from Global CAD Solutions." That's a SUMIFS with three criteria. Syntax: SUMIFS(sum range, criteria range 1, criteria 1, criteria range 2, criteria 2...).

2:30 — PivotTables summarize large data sets fastest. Project Code to rows, Vendor Name to columns, Amount to values — in 10 seconds you have a summary that would take an hour of formulas.

3:30 — In the Excel lab, raw offshore drafting data — 1,200 rows. Tag project codes with VLOOKUP. Sum by project with SUMIFS. Present with a PivotTable. All three.

chunks.title: Data cleaning, VLOOKUP/XLOOKUP, and SUMIFS
chunks.bodyHtml:
Clean data is the accountant's first problem. Raw exports arrive with combined date-description fields, inconsistent vendor names, trailing spaces, and stray characters. Before any analysis you standardize the data.

The skill set, with Skarion data:

ToolWhat it doesSkarion use
Text-to-ColumnsSplits a combined field (e.g. "2026-01-14 Atlantic Fiber wire") on a delimiter into separate columns.Cleaning bank-export CSVs where date and description share one cell.
TRIM / PROPER / CLEANRemoves extra spaces, fixes capitalization, strips non-printable characters.Standardizing "GLOBAL CAD SOLUTIONS LTD." vs "Global Cad Solutions" so SUMIFS actually matches.
VLOOKUP / XLOOKUPPull a matching value from a master table.Matching 3,000 rows of offshore labor hours to project codes in a master table.
SUMIFSSum a range across multiple conditions (project + date + vendor).Totaling COGS for the Tennessee Corridor for January across all vendors.
IF / IFSFlag rows that meet a rule.Flagging offshore invoices where the unit price exceeds the MSA rate of $38.50/mile.
Conditional FormattingColor cells that meet a rule, automatically.Highlighting 90+ day AR balances ($34,200 of TeleCom Builders) in red.

chunks.title: The messy offshore timesheet export
chunks.instructionsHtml:
Download the raw export below — a 1,200-row CSV of individual CAD and GIS drafting tasks from 3 offshore firms across 8 projects, including the Tennessee Corridor build.

  1. Text-to-Columns to split the combined date/description field.
  2. TRIM/PROPER/CLEAN to standardize vendor names so they match the master project table.
  3. XLOOKUP or VLOOKUP to attach the correct Project Code to every row from the project mapping table.
  4. SUMIFS to compute total drafting labor cost per project.
  5. Deliverable: report the total Tennessee Corridor drafting cost for the month.
chunks.reflectionPrompt: What was the total drafting cost for the Tennessee Corridor project? Which vendor had the largest share, and did any invoices exceed the $38.50/mile MSA rate?

chunks.title: PivotTables, Power Query, and Excel interview questions
chunks.bodyHtml:
PivotTables are the fastest way to summarize 10,000 rows. Drag-and-drop grouping, summing, and filtering by date/project/vendor — no formulas required. For month-end, a PivotTable turns a raw transaction export into an AP aging view in minutes.

Power Query (Get & Transform) automates the monthly cleanup so you are not manually running Text-to-Columns every close. You build the transformation once; each month you refresh and the cleaned dataset lands in your model. This is how real close cycles stop being manual.

Lab 2 — AP Aging PivotTable: a raw AP transaction export with 800 rows, 6 vendors, 4 months of invoices. Build a PivotTable showing outstanding AP by vendor Aging bucket (0–30, 31–60, 61–90, 90+), then identify which vendor carries the most overdue balance and assess the risk.

chunks.title: AP Aging PivotTable lab
chunks.instructionsHtml:
Download the raw AP transaction export below and build an AP aging PivotTable.

  1. Load the export into a PivotTable with Vendor on rows and an aging bucket (0–30, 31–60, 61–90, 90+) on columns, summing the outstanding invoice balance.
  2. Add conditional formatting so the 90+ day buckets highlight in red.
  3. Identify the vendor with the largest 90+ day balance and write a one-line risk assessment for the controller.
chunks.reflectionPrompt: Which vendor carried the most overdue (90+ day) balance, and what is the dollar amount and the collection risk you'd flag to the controller?

chunks.title: 8 Excel interview questions — and real answers

cards.back: XLOOKUP defaults to an exact match (no trailing FALSE), can look left of the lookup column, and can return arrays. VLOOKUP is older, can only look right, and silently returns approximate matches if you forget the FALSE.

cards.front: How do you handle #N/A in VLOOKUP?
cards.back: Wrap with IFERROR or IFNA: =IFERROR(VLOOKUP(...), 0) or =IFNA(VLOOKUP(...), "no match"). Decide whether a missing match should be zero (sum-safe) or flagged.

cards.front: When use SUMIFS over a PivotTable?
cards.back: When the result needs to feed another formula live, or land in a fixed template cell a controller reads. PivotTables are great for exploration; SUMIFS is great for repeatable, formula-driven reports.

cards.front: How do you lock a cell reference?
cards.back: $A$1 locks both column and row (absolute); A1 locks neither (relative); $A1 locks column only, A$1 locks row only (mixed). Toggle with F4.

cards.front: What is a named range and when is it useful?
cards.back: A readable alias for a cell or range, e.g. MSA_Rate for a single cell. Useful in formulas that many people read =IF(UnitPrice>MSA_Rate,"HOLD"). Self-documenting and survives row insertions if defined dynamically.

cards.front: How do you compare two lists to find missing values?
cards.back: XLOOKUP for each item in list A against list B — #N/A means missing. Or COUNTIF(range, value) = 0. Filter the #N/As to see exactly what's in one list but not the other.

cards.front: What does Power Query do that manual cleaning doesn't?
cards.back: It records the transformation steps and replays them on refresh — so a monthly Text-to-Columns + TRIM + lookup pipeline runs every month with one click, not a manual rebuild. It is the difference between cleaning data and automating the cleaning.

cards.front: How do you prevent someone editing your formula columns?
cards.back: Lock the cells (Format Cells > Protection > Locked), then protect the sheet (Review > Protect Sheet). Locking alone does nothing until sheet protection is turned on.

chunks.intro: A quick check on the Excel skills you'll be tested on in interviews and on day one.
chunks.title: Excel knowledge check

questions.q: Why is XLOOKUP generally preferred over VLOOKUP in modern accounting?
options: It defaults to an exact match and can return values to the left of the lookup column
It uses less memory than VLOOKUP
questions.explanation: XLOOKUP defaults to exact match (no trailing FALSE argument) and can return arrays to the left of the lookup value. VLOOKUP can only look right and silently returns approximate matches if you omit FALSE.

questions.q: You need the total COGS for the Tennessee Corridor project across all vendors for January. Which function do you reach for?
questions.explanation: SUMIFS sums a range across multiple conditions — here project = Tennessee Corridor AND month = January AND category = COGS. That multi-condition summing is exactly what SUMIFS is for.

questions.q: Which cell reference locks the column but lets the row change as you fill down?
questions.explanation: $A1 locks the column (the $ before A) but lets the row adjust as the formula is filled down. Toggle reference types with F4.

questions.q: What is the single biggest advantage of Power Query over manual data cleaning?
options: It runs faster on large files
It records transformation steps and replays them on refresh, so monthly cleanup is one click
It writes the journal entries for you
It replaces the need for PivotTables
questions.explanation: Power Query captures your cleanup steps and replays them every month on refresh — turning a manual Text-to-Columns + TRIM + lookup routine into a repeatable, automated pipeline. That automation is the whole point.

chunks.title: AI Tools in Modern Accounting — What's Real, What's Hype
chunks.bodyHtml:
AI tools have moved from marketing into actual accounting workflows in 2025–2026. Knowing where they genuinely help — and where they don't — is now a real interview topic.

What AI is good at now:

  • Categorizing transactions (QuickBooks' AI suggestions, Sage Intacct's AI features)
  • Matching invoices to POs at scale
  • Flagging anomalies in large datasets (duplicate-looking amounts, out-of-pattern vendors)
  • Auto-populating expense reports from receipt images
  • Drafting first-pass reconciliation explanations and journal entry memos (with ChatGPT/Copilot)

What AI is not good at:

  • Exercising accounting judgment — when to hold, correct, document, or escalate
  • Catching context-dependent fraud (a transaction that looks normal but isn't, given the business relationship)
  • GAAP compliance decisions that require reading the contract and the business intent
  • Signing off. AI does not sign off. You do.

Tools worth knowing: Sage Intacct's AI features, QuickBooks AI categorization, Microsoft Copilot for Excel, ChatGPT for first-draft memos. The safe position for a junior accountant: AI handles the volume work, you handle the judgment calls and documentation.

callout.html: Interview positioning: "I use AI tools to handle the mechanical volume of data preparation, which lets me spend more time on judgment-intensive work like reconciliation investigation and adjusting-entry review." That answer shows you understand the line — and you will be asked it.

chunks.title: 5 AI accounting tools — what each does and can't

cards.back: Suggests account categories for bank-feed transactions from historical patterns. Can't tell when a transaction is genuinely unusual or a one-off — review every flagged exception, don't auto-accept. Interview line: "I use QB's AI suggestions as a first pass, then review exceptions manually."

Automates transaction matching, anomaly flagging, and some reconciliations at scale. Still requires a human sign-off on accruals and judgment entries. Can't interpret the business reason behind a variance.

Drafts formulas, builds PivotTables, summarizes ranges, cleans data on instruction. Useful for first drafts and bulk clean-up. Can't validate that the underlying accounting treatment is correct — it's a formula tool, not a GAAP tool.

Drafts reconciliation explanations and journal-entry memos from raw facts. Excellent first draft. Cannot verify the numbers are right or decide when an adjusting entry is actually needed — always verify before posting.
Reads receipts and auto-populates expense reports. Great volume work. Still needs human review for category coding, policy compliance, and duplicate detection.

chunks.title: Scenario Lab — The Excel Clean-Up Assignment From Hell
chunks.intro: The hardest Excel exercise in the course. A 3,000-row offshore labor export with eight deliberate quality problems. Fix all eight, produce clean data, and build the project profitability summary.
chunks.setup:
You receive Offshore_Labor_Raw_Q1_2026.csv — 3,000 rows of individual CAD and GIS task entries from 4 offshore firms across 12 projects. Eight deliberate quality problems are baked in:

  1. Date column is a single text field like "Jan-15-2026" — needs Text-to-Columns.
  2. Vendor names have inconsistent spacing ("Global CAD Solutions " with a trailing space).
  3. Duplicate entries — the same task ID entered twice for two vendors.
  4. Project codes have typos ("Proj-Alpha" vs "Proj Alpha" vs "ProjAlpha").
  5. One column uses a comma as a decimal separator (European format) — needs conversion to US format.
  6. Three rows have $0 amounts but non-zero hours — a fee-structure error to investigate.
  7. A VLOOKUP to the project master fails for 47 rows due to a case mismatch.
  8. The billing rate column has mixed currency ($, £, €) for different offshore offices.
tasks: Download the raw CSV and the project master mapping below.

Fix all eight problems in order, documenting the tool used for each (Text-to-Columns, TRIM/PROPER/CLEAN, Remove Duplicates, Find/Replace for project codes, comma-to-decimal conversion, FLAG vs. fix the $0/hours rows in writing, EXACT/CASE match for the VLOOKUP, currency conversion to USD).
Run a clean VLOOKUP to attach the project code to every row.
Use SUMIFS to total dollar cost and hours per project.
Build a PivotTable showing hours and cost by project and by vendor.
Identify which project has the highest cost overrun vs. its budget and write a one-line flag.
chunks.submissionPrompt: Paste: (1) the tool you used for each of the 8 problems, (2) your final cleaned row count, (3) the Tennessee Corridor and Texas Long-Haul totals (hours and $), and (4) the project with the largest cost overrun + your one-line flag.
chunks.solution:
Eight fixes (tools): (1) Text-to-Columns on the date field, delimiter "-"; (2) TRIM + PROPER on vendor names, then Remove Duplicates on Task ID + Date + Vendor; (3) Remove Duplicates or pivot-count to surface duplicate task IDs; (4) Find/Replace + a helper column with a normalized key (spaces/dashes stripped) to unify the project codes; (5) SUBSTITUTE(",",".") or value-conversion on the comma-decimal column; (6) Flag the three $0/positive-hours rows in writing — likely a rate-missing error, do NOT silently zero-fill; route to the offshore PM for confirmation; (7) use UPPER() on both the lookup key and the master to fix the case mismatch, then VLOOKUP succeeds for all 47; (8) multiply the billing-rate column by each offshore office's currency-to-USD rate and store a single USD column.

Clean row count ≈ 3,000 minus the duplicates removed (typically ~2,984 after the 16 duplicate rows are stripped).

Tennessee Corridor should be the highest-volume project (hundreds of hours, the largest $ total — the dominant share of Q1 offshore drafting). Texas Long-Haul is the second-largest by hours.

Cost-overrun flag: the project whose SUMIFS total exceeds the master-table budget for that project — flag it as "Over budget — review with PM before next PO revision." The exact overrun dollar figure depends on the seeded data, but the comparison method is the deliverable.
